data.title: Snap-On Incorporated: Expensive With Limited Catalyst Visibility
data.description:
Snap-on Incorporated (NYSE:SNA) is a manufacturer and distributor of professional tools, diagnostic software, and repair solutions for automotive and industrial technicians through its proprietary direct-to-customer network. I initiate on SNA with a Sell rating, as SNA's 11.45x CY26e EV/EBITDA valuation multiple is at the 75th percentile of its 3-year range. Using my mean reversion approach to valuation, SNA's 0.44x premium to its historical average multiple is fully rolled off by CY26e, supporting my $218.39 target price and -31% downside potential. SNA's Tools Group shift into 'quick-payback' hand and power tools should generate incremental sales/mix, albeit in a tough macro environment, which supports my CY26e EBITDA forecast of $1.012 billion (21.0% margin). However, significant pricing pressure from value competitors as well as technician budget constraints will likely limit Snap-on's margin opportunity. Therefore, I believe that with Tools Group execution largely modeled in and without a step up in new vehicle or technician demand or improvement opportunities, Snap-on's shares have limited further multiple upside.

RS&I Intelligent Diagnostics Drives Recurring Revenue and Margin Upside

With increasing EV and ADAS complexity driving the need for more sophisticated vehicle diagnostics, I expect SNA's RS&I segment to provide a catalyst in the coming months as it grows the APOLLO+ and Triton intelligent diagnostic systems business. As SNA has discussed this new strategic initiative in its recent earnings calls and investor events, I expect management to begin commenting on initial traction on those products in its upcoming earnings calls.

I also think SNA will highlight the early success of its subscription attach rate, which increases its addressable market to DIY customers and non-typical auto repair shops, and overall subscription units, which move RS&I's revenue mix toward higher-margin recurring revenue. Investors will look for metrics around the product placements and subscription attach rates as early indicators to help model the embedded growth opportunity.

Given the potential for SNA to point to tangible momentum indicators in RS&I, I believe there is an element of positive surprise around RS&I revenue, subscription revenue, and EBITDA growth in the coming quarters. This is particularly relevant given the headwinds experienced by the Tools group in recent quarters, as I believe incremental software subscription revenue could help offset margin pressure as technicians are under increasing pressure to purchase more value-oriented tools in their constrained budgets.

If SNA is able to demonstrate strong momentum in increasing subscription rates and other software sales metrics, I believe this could point to performance that comes in above current consensus estimates in RS&I revenue and EBITDA, leading to upside to my current estimates. This, in turn, could result in slightly offsetting the current downturn in Tools segment EBITDA caused by pricing pressures, potentially leading to an upside for overall segment profitability.

With analysts consequently likely to raise estimates based on this momentum, this could drive a re-rating of SNA's EV/EBITDA multiple toward its historical average as investors react positively to the recurring revenue story, providing the catalyst to close SNA's valuation discount to its historical average.

Quick-Payback Tool Adoption Is The Key Focus For Near-Term Sales/Margins Upside

SNA's pivot to quick-payback tools should continue to be a key sales attributer over the next few months. I expect Q3 results to shed more light on the adoption of quick payback tools among 'cash rich but confidence poor' technicians.

While I anticipate the commentary to provide reasons for optimism on the outlook for the Tools group, I believe investors will want to pay attention to the year-over-hand tool/power tool category growth rates in proprietary van, diagnostic tool attach rates and feedback around more frequent purchase decision-making on the quick payback tools and lower priced alternatives (Craftsman, Gearwrench, Tekton, etc.).

Furthermore, I expect management to address whether the improving product mix and technician feedback indicate that Snap-on can sustain premium pricing in exchange for demonstrable productivity benefits.

Bottom line: I expect sequential tools revenue to have accelerated 3–5% QoQ, given the uplift from quick payback tools, which can drive 20–30bps of incremental tools margin. SNA is leveraging its fixed costs vs. increasing volume through quick payback tools.

A better than expected performance driven by quick payback tool momentum and SNA convincing the market that its premium pricing is stickier or the market should expect to see benefits from SNA's better pricing ability would be positive for SNA vs my CY26e largely stable EBITDA numbers ($1,012m, 21.0% margins).

Finally SNA's ability to convince the market that it has the tools that appeal to the D2T technicians primarily towards the productivity improvements would be key for SNA's qualitative story around D2T, and I believe it would be a driver of multiple expansion.

Commercial & Industrial Custom-Torque & Kit Momentum To Support Segment Sustainability

Snap-on's focus on specialty tools and kits for Aerospace, Military and Energy customers paves the way for in-line mid-single-digit growth in C&I organically likely over the next 12–24 months. In these markets, precise application and traceability are extremely important, and SNA's turnkey kits and solutions with digital reporting embedded address exactly that.

Investors should be on the lookout for C&I order book expansion, commentary on new programme launches and ASP trends on future earnings calls as leading indicators of C&I traction. I expect this catalyst to drive about ~25–50bps of margin tailwinds through mix uplift following higher-value product bundles, helping to offset the pricing pressure Tools Group has experienced the last 1–2 years.

Although a longer cycle nature of sales may introduce some volatility here, a sustained cadence of new project wins in Aerospace and D&E will validate SNA's moat and de-risk my CY26e EBITDA margins (21.0%) marginally.

While I note growth in C&I can serve as a valuable stabilizer for the near term, in my view, absent a broader improvement in tech demand, this is unlikely to drive multiple expansion in my thesis for a Sell.

What Could Drive The Stock Even Lower?

My investment thesis on Snap-on is predicated on the mix shift into higher-margin diagnostic software and the WC-driven FCF tailwind that support the 11.0x EV/EBITDA valuation. Which I think is already expensive. I've identified three company-specific risks that could threaten the multiple to go even lower:

First, a slowdown in vehicle repair activity that could result from slowing miles driven, fast EV penetration (lower need for repairs), or slower ADAS/electrification development could pressure software penetration and, in turn, the 100 bps mix shift that I model, which drives 50 bps of margin expansion.

Second credit stress among its franchisees and loss of route could threaten its cash-flow execution. Underperforming dealers could defer purchases or could default on loans, increasing the risk of credit-loss provisions, rising DSO, and loss of the ~£100m annual WC tailwind I model for CY26.

Third competition from low-cost tool manufacturers and/or underperformance of its new hand- and power-tool launches could pressure productivity and pricing power, threatening my 21.0% CY26e EBITDA margin and exposing SNA to multiple compression.

Risks To My Sell Thesis

We sort of have to think about everything in reverse here. Improved traction vehicle repair could drive multiple expansion and be fundamentally a problem to my sell thesis. Overall improvements and easing by the Fed can cause general expansion in multiples that, again, without substantial underlying business performance improvement, cause havoc to my rating.

Overall investors closely watch the factors mentioned in what could drive the stock lower. As both upside and downside potential to this thesis.

Conclusion

I'm bearish as Snap-on's CY26e EV/EBITDA multiple (11.45x) would sit at a multiyear high with limited catalyst visibility. At the core of my bearish thesis is the idea that rising software margins from mix shift into higher-margin diagnostics and quick-payback tools are already priced into the stock. What I will be looking for: RS&I subscription attach rates as well as broader technician demand indicators for signs of upside or broader levels of downside.

data.title: Nebius Group: The New AI Champion
data.description:
In early July, we issued a second public Buy rating on Nebius Group N.V. (NASDAQ:NBIS) at $46. Shares are up 46% from there at the time of this writing. Of course, today shares are up about 20% following a well-received Q2 earnings report.

We continue to view Nebius as a long-term winner. Right now a lot of traditional valuation metrics do not make sense to use here, so, given it is a hypergrowth name, we are looking for more revenue, more customers, and moves more in the direction toward less cash burn, to translate to more gains. You see this a lot with growth tech companies. This can continue for a few more quarters, but at some point the market will want to see a move to true profit for this company with its huge focus on AI infrastructure.

Right now, a lot of AI infrastructure plays are popular with the market and we see that continuing, outside some possible seasonal weakness. This company recognizes the need for large-scale GPUs and cloud platforms while having strategic partner businesses working on generative AI development, tech career skill building, and autonomous vehicle tech. The Street is upbeat. In this article, we update on the hypergrowth name. Let us discuss.

Q2 results were strong, big recurring revenue

In the just reported Q2 earnings, Nebius reported revenue of $105 million, a huge sequential increase from the $55 million in Q1. Further, it increased by an incredible 625% year-over-year. This was a beat of $4 million against estimates. Just huge growth. This is why we call it 'hypergrowth.' Such revenue growth stemmed from investments in its core AI infrastructure business. Now we have annualized recurring revenue pushing $1 billion. This is huge for the company, and was something we had opined was in the cards for the company. The guide for the year was $900 million to $1.1 billion. To be clear and balanced, if it comes in on the low end, this could be taken as a negative, so, we are looking for $1 billion plus to keep the stock rally going in the medium-term.

High costs, losses from operations

While we continue to see huge growth in revenue, it is worth noting that this comes with huge increases in operating costs and revenues. This is one negative to be aware of. And shorts are betting against the company. But that is part of why news like today's earnings results in big rallies. However, we are seeing one positive here, and that is the there was swing to positive net income this quarter which was huge, and the loss from operations was actually $1 million less than a year ago. Year to date, loss from operations is about $50 million higher than the comparable period a year ago, but we are seeing positive news.

In Q2, the cost of revenue was up to $30.1 million from $7.7million last year. The product development costs were $42.8 million, rising from $32.0 million. Sales and admin expenses were however well controlled at to $68.2 million, down from $75.6 million last year. There as some big depreciation costs, up to $75.2 million, versus $11.4 million last year. And so, total operating costs and expenses nearly doubled from last year to $216.3 million, so, there was a $111.2 million loss from operations. But this was down $1 million from last year.

Generally speaking, Nebius loses some serious money. However, down line on the income from operations statement, we saw a gain from a revaluation of its equity investments. That was good for $597.4 million. So that led to positive net income of $584.4 million. Of course adjusting for this and other expenses, we saw adjusted EBITDA loss of $21.0 million. The good news? This was down 64% from a year ago. But adjusted net loss increased 49% to $91.5 million. So, it is losing more money now than it was a year ago. Eventually, the market will care about this. For now, it is about growing those revenues.

Cash is in good shape right now, but still burning cash

Cash and equivalents at the end of the quarter were $1.67 billion, down $755 million from the start of the year. Remember the company took in $2.5 billion from a divestment last year and got a $700 million investment total from high-level investors a few months ago. But it is still burning cash. OF course, cash was up from Q1, but that was because they entered in another round of debt. In June, the company entered into an agreement for the private placement of senior unsecured convertible notes, totaling $1 billion, with two tranches, both $500 million each.

So, Nebius is burning money to build out its AI business, and is actively exploring new sites. Keep in mind again that it went from having just one location in Finland, to now five in Europe, the United States, and the Middle East, with plans for much more expansion. This is positive.

Looking ahead

We still have a hypergrowth name here, and it is all about revenues, and the annualized recurring revenue. We got a strong outlook for $1 billion on this metrics at the midpoint. This has been seen as bullish.

The revenue growth is outpacing the growth of operating expenses, which is positive, but the company is still losing money and burning cash. So, it has a few quarters of cash to burn, but likely will issue more debt in early 2026, something to be aware of, just given how much the company is investing in its growth. Overall, it is tough to chase Nebius Group N.V. stock here, but we remain bullish on the long term.

data.title: Elanco Animal Health Q2: Rising Innovation Revenue, Initiate At Buy
data.description:
Elanco Animal Health Incorporated (NYSE:ELAN) develops drugs to prevent and treat disease in farm animals and pets, competing with Zoetis (ZTS), the industry leader in animal pharmaceuticals. I believe the company’s focus on innovative products could potentially diversify its product categories in both companion and farm animal markets. Elanco posted a strong quarterly result and raised its full-year guidance. I am initiating with a Buy rating with a fair value of $20 per share.

Similar to Zoetis, Elanco develops and manufactures parasiticides, vaccines and therapeutics for companion animals, and medicated feed additives, injectable antibiotics and vaccines for farm animals. Due to its small scale, the company has focused on select innovative products such as Zenrelia and Credelio Quattro, as shown in the slide below. For instance, Elanco received FDA clearance for Zenrelia, a JAK inhibitor targeting control of pruritus and atopic dermatitis in dogs, in 2024, as noted in its 10K. The Zenrelia market share has increased form only 2% this March to 4% in June, with more than 10,000 clinics starting to use this products. As an animal pharmaceutical company, innovative drug launches are crucial to its future growth, in my view.

Elanco has strategically divested non-core business to improve its capital allocation and focus on pet health and livestock. On July 9th, 2024, the company divested its aqua business to Merck Animal Health for $1.294 billion. The company used the proceeds to pay down its debt. As the aqua business provides products for warm-water and cold-water species, there are no synergies between its aqua business and companion and farm animals market. I view the divestiture makes strategic sense.

Elanco released its Q2 FY25 result on August 7th, posting 8% organic revenue growth with $0.26 of adjusted EPS. As detailed in the table below, the company delivered both price and volume growth for the quarter, across both pet health and farm animal segments.

I think it is impressive to see the management raised its innovation revenue target to $720 - $800 million for the full year, representing around 17% of total revenues. The innovation business is very important for the company to turn around its overall organic growth and gain market share in both companion and farm animal spaces. As depicted in the slide below, the company has gradually improved its innovations over the past few years, a strategic move to reaccelerate its overall growth.

Due to its strong quarterly result, the company raised its full-year guidance as detailed in the slide below:

Business Research Insights forecasts the global animal pharmaceutical market will grow at a CAGR of 5.13% from 2025 to 2033, driven by rising awareness of animal health and well-being. With more innovative drug launches, I think Elanco can at least grow in line with the overall market. For FY25, I forecast the company will grow its revenue by 6%, in line with its guidance. From FY26 onwards, I anticipate a 7.2% total revenue growth, assuming 5% organic growth and 2.2% M&A growth. I estimate the company could spend 3% of revenue on M&A, contributing to an additional 2.2% growth to the topline.

Elanco has been actively restructuring its operations to reduce overhead costs and optimize its global supply chain. They have spent a total of $460 million on restructurings over the past three years, which could support a margin improvement in the near future. I forecast the company will deliver 50bps annual margin improvement assuming 20bps from new products mix; 20bps from reduction in SG&A and 10bps from operating leverage of sales and marketing expenses. I calculate the total operating costs will grow by 6.6% annually.

The DCF can be summarized as follows:

The FCFF is calculated below:

The WACC is estimated to be 8.1% assuming risk free 4.2%; beta 1.1; equity risk premium 6%; cost of debt 5%; equity $7 billion; debt $4.3 billion; tax rate 25%. I set the terminal growth rate to be 4%, assuming 2% pricing growth and 2% volume growth.

Discounting all the future FCFF and adjusting net debt, the fair value is calculated to be $20 per share, as per my estimate.

Key Risk

As shown in the slide below, Elanco exited the quarter with 4x net debt leverage, a pretty high level in my view. While the company plans to reduce it leverage below 3x over time, the company’s high debt level would limit its capacity for shares buyback and strategic acquisitions.

As noted in its 10K, Elanco has customer concentration risks, with a third-party veterinary distributor, an affiliate of Cencora, representing 11% of total revenues. If the distributor decides to switch to anther manufacturer, Elanco’s growth will be significantly affected.

Lastly, the company has $4.4 billion of goodwill on its balance sheet, which could face future impairment charges. For instance, the company charged $1.042 billion to goodwill and intangible impairment in FY23 due to rising interest rate. The amount of goodwill is quite significant compared to its market cap of $7.05 billion.

Conclusion

Elanco Animal Health is in the process of launching more innovative products in both companion and farm animal markets, which could potentially accelerate its overall revenue growth. I am initiating with a Buy rating with a fair value of $20 per share.

data.title: AMD: Record-Breaking Q2, But Don't Buy Until $156
data.description:
When I last covered Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (NASDAQ:AMD) on November 4th, 2024 with my article “AMD: Buy The Drop”, the stock was experiencing a strong build-up in selling pressure and leaving many investors wondering where the bottom in share prices would likely be found. Unfortunately for bulls, there was still more pain to be seen because the stock did not print a terminal trough until markets traded below the $76.50 level. Since then, recovery trends have been quite impressive and AMD shares have been able to break important historical resistance levels and outperform the gains made in the S&P 500 during the same period of time. Now that investors have a new set of earnings and guidance figures to analyze, I will explain why I am maintaining my “strong buy” rating on this stock and the reasoning behind my expectation that a modest post-earnings crash might create new buying opportunities near the middle $150s.

First, I think it is important for traders to make an assessment of whether or not downward is the most appropriate short-term direction following AMD’s second quarter earnings release. To be fair, there are some valid arguments that can be made in both directions. Given the sell-off we are seeing, it seems fair to assume that the market is focused on the EPS miss ($0.48 per share versus the $0.49 consensus estimates from LSEG) but I think the deeper issue here is that business revenue from AMD’s artificial intelligence GPUs actually came in negatively on an annualized basis (even though revenue from the data center segment saw gains of 14%). But what should be understood is that a lot of this information was already known in advance: U.S. government restrictions forced MI308 chip sales to essentially grind to a halt in China and AMD is also in the process of transitioning to its next-generation product lines. Market demand for AMD’s EPYC server CPUs still remains quite strong, however, and if we do see future approvals for Chinese licenses I think operating margins have the potential to move into positive territory (either during the third or fourth quarter of this year).

On the positive side, AMD managed to generate record free cash flows of $1.2 billion (even with these geopolitical headwinds) and this figure puts AMD in an excellent position when compared to industry competitors like Intel Corp. (INTC) and ARM Holdings (ARM). AMD also reduced its total debt figure to $3.2 billion and repurchased $478 million worth of common stock. Overall, this was AMD’s best second-quarter sales performance on record (with $7.685 billion in revenue) and this performance marks an annualized revenue gain of 32% relative to the figures we saw at this point last year. In addition to all of this, quarterly net income spiked sharply to $0.54 per share (at $872 million) and this gives us further evidence of undeniable strength when we compare these results to the $0.16 per share print ($265 million) that we saw in Q2 2024.

Next, we will quickly run through some comparative valuation metrics to get a better sense of which stocks likely represent the best buying opportunity right now. In the chart above, one aspect that should stand out is the fact that AMD’s forward PE ratio of 41.23x is roughly in-line with what is seen with Nvidia Corp. (NVDA) at 41.7x. Of course, AMD still doesn’t compare to the cash flow-generating juggernaut that is Nvidia, so there are obviously very fair arguments which suggest that NVDA is still a better buy right now. However, investors that are strongly bullish on AMD might counter with the argument that the stock’s forward PEG ratio is a more important valuation metric. AMD’s 0.139x forward PEG ratio is the cheapest in this entire group.

One aspect that has kept me from adding more to my AMD long position (and the main reason I believe investors should wait for a bit more of a drop toward the middle $150s) is that AMD’s forward EV/EBITDA metric is far too elevated. This valuation perspective certainly puts names like Qualcomm, Inc. (QCOM) in a much more favorable light because that stock currently trades with a bizarrely low forward EV/EBITDA ratio of just 9.6x. Intel and Supermicro Computer, Inc. (SMCI) are also trading toward the lower end of the range, but both of those stocks have a much more negative narrative overhang that might (understandably) keep some investors on the sidelines.

Given the massive ramp-up that we have seen in artificial intelligence spending over the last three years, we should also assess these stocks based on their return on invested capital metrics because this aspect of the equation does provide us with another risk element which suggests that recent AMD share price declines might not be done just yet. When viewing the group based on this valuation metric, Nvidia is still claiming its position as “belle of the ball”, and this is why I simply cannot understand why we see so many arguments suggesting that AMD is a better buy than NVDA. At the very least, I think investors should be looking at this group to design a portfolio basket of stocks that offer exposure to several of these names at the same time. I suppose that if I had to buy just one stock, it would be NVDA but that is a hypothetical restraint that no investor should be placing upon themselves.

One other news-based aspect of this story revolves around U.S. President Donald Trump’s latest policy proposal designed to impose 100% tariffs on companies importing semiconductors. These proposals will offer exemptions for companies that make substantive commitments to manufacture semiconductors in the United States, and President Trump has already mentioned that Nvidia qualifies for a more favorable status based on the company’s relationship with Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSM) at its Arizona-based foundry.

Nvidia has made public commitments to produce substantial volume (as much as $500 billion in artificial intelligence hardware) for its more advanced chips at fabs located in the United States, and it is relatively clear that CEO Jensen Huang has achieved “first-mover” status in this regard. At the moment, Micron Technology (MU), Texas Instruments (TXN) also appear to be well-positioned for potential tariff exemption status once U.S. fabs are built, but the current situation still looks less certain for AMD.

In April 2025, CEO Lisa Su announced that AMD plans to have "key processors" produced at TSMC-Arizona, but these comments lack specifics and we are seeing very little in terms of concrete timelines, dollar figures, or wafer volume numbers at the moment. If we do not soon see a more binding agreement from AMD showing that the company aims to ramp-up wafer production in the U.S., there are risks that regulators in the U.S. could classify AMD as an importer relatively soon. If this were to occur, my estimates suggest that newly imposed tariffs could put a squeeze on gross margins and increase AMD’s bill of materials (BOM) by as much as 4-6 percentage points on every chip manufactured in Taiwan.

If you are bullish on this stock, what you want to see would be a more formal production commitment at TSMC Arizona (or even a new partnership with an Intel foundry) because this would then have the potential to neutralize these developing tariff policy measures (to a large extent). As a result, this is one aspect of the story that should be viewed as an incrementally negative downside risk that could be alleviated by public comments from AMD suggesting that a clearer timeline for U.S. production has been reached.

Looking into the price chart history, we can see that recent resistance breaks have placed AMD in elevated territory from the historical perspective. Pullbacks from the July 31st highs of $182.50 have already broken through the 20-day exponential moving average and started to see modest bounces from the 23.6% Fibonacci retracement of the rally from $76.48 (which is located near $157.60). Daily indicator readings in the relative strength index are clearly rolling over out of overbought territory and this suggests that there is still potential for the stock to continue declining before we reach oversold levels once again. Right now, I am looking for a test of the 50-day exponential moving average before buying more AMD stock because this price chart is currently showing all of the major characteristics of a topping formation without much evidence that downside momentum has started to slow.

Conclusion

On balance, I would recommend exercising some patience at this stage. It is very easy to start making the argument that we should be “buying the dip”, but this type of activity is often a knee-jerk reaction that results in premature entry points. Long-term, I still believe that AMD’s bull thesis remains intact. This is a company that is producing record-breaking free cash flow figures, possesses an immaculate balance sheet, and continues to assert its dominance in both server and desktop CPUs. For whatever reason, this is also a company that has been punished by external headline risks (with the MI308 write-downs and export bans to China being the most recent examples). But there are still risks in AMD’s valuation metric profile that need to be understood and this is why exercising a bit more patience here is still the most prudent move.

data.title: Nebius Group: The Post-Earnings Surge Is Just The Ignition Sequence
data.description:
My Thesis Update On Nebius Group

All Nebius Group N.V.'s (NASDAQ:NBIS) bulls must now be on a huge wave of happiness after the firm reported for its fiscal Q2 FY2025 a few hours ago, and the stock is surging up by over 20% (at the time of this writing). If you read my articles for some time, you probably know that I was extremely bullish on Nebius' ability to execute on the plans the management set for itself. So, I reiterated my "Strong Buy" rating last month when I was previewing the Q2 quarter results.

The upcoming Q2 earnings report is a critical catalyst. Strong results should force analysts to revise their inexplicably low estimates, closing the valuation gap.

Source: From my July article on NBIS.

Since that call of mine, NBIS stock is effectively up by almost 40% (in about a month).

The underlying reasons are what I'll discuss below, but from what I see today, I think that the post-earnings surge may be just the beginning of something bigger for NBIS shareholders. Yes, some limits seem to be flowing from the valuation stretch, but I don't think that growth AI investors should think about valuation as something that should be put in the first place of priority when it comes to financial analysis. NBIS isn't a "Strong Buy," I agree, but it's still a great Buy for the long term, in my opinion.

Reviewing Nebius Group's Q2 2025 Results

Nebius Group's Q2 print showed a headline revenue figure of $105.1 million (+625% YoY), beating the consensus estimate by over 3%, according to Seeking Alpha. But it wasn't the main bullish news that led to such a severe buying pressure amid the falling broader stock market: Nebius' CEO actually said on the earnings call that they were "oversold on all of [their] supply of previous generation hoppers," and so effectively, the top-line expansion could have been even stronger than what they reported. In other words, NBIS's growth story may be limited by only the existing supply constraints right now, while the overall underlying AI demand is extremely strong, and it seems to get stronger from quarter to quarter.

The firm's bottom line loss keeps expanding for now, but it's taking place amid the massive improvement on the adjusted EBITDA level: In Q2, NBIS improved this metric to negative $21 million versus Q2 FY2024's EBITDA loss of -$58.1 million. So, that's why the EPS loss expansion doesn't bother the market just yet; if the top-line growth remains at a solid level, and the EBITDA keeps moving in the right direction, then it means that NBIS does everything correctly. And the management's updated ARR guidance says that the should be even brighter than some bulls previously expected. I mean, they raised the FY2025 ARR range to $900-1,100 million, with the arrival of Nvidia's (NVDA) new Blackwell GPUs being the catalyst that should help them unlock the next wave of growth.

Although the GPU installations are heavily back-end weighted to Q4, and this massive ARR upgrade didn't translate to a higher FY2025 revenue internal expectation, I'd expect a meaningful revenue acceleration throughout fiscal 2026. And it doesn't seem to be correctly reflected in today's revenue expectations just yet because there were no revisions over the past month, based on Seeking Alpha.

A logical thing to expect now is an avalanche of positive revisions made to the firm's forward sales figures. As before, the updated guidance confirmed that NBISI should break even on an adjusted EBITDA basis by the end of 2025 and stay in the positive territory for the entire 2026. With their total cost of ownership (TCO) being ~20% lower compared to peers, which is a competitive advantage in itself, it is what makes me kind of confident that they'll reach their profitability targets relatively easily.

So, the core AI infrastructure business, what NBIS is mostly known for, seems to be doing even better than what the high hopes of the market participants were anticipating. Management also pointed to some comps for their portfolio companies that suggest immense underlying value: Scale AI's $30 billion valuation as a comp for Toloka; Waymo's $40-50 billion valuation for their autonomous vehicle unit Avride; and ClickHouse's recent capital raise at a reported $6 billion valuation. So, based on their estimation, their non-core portfolio adds "a potential source of "several billion dollars," and I see some potential synergies along the way that should value NBIS at a premium for longer.

With all that in mind, let's jump to the stock's valuation to handle the question, "How much is left for the newcomers?"

Nebius Valuation: Great Stock Comes At A Cost

Again, I can't call NBIS a bargain stock after its huge run-up since the February meltdown, but from what I see, the multiple contraction priced in today kind of explains why the market overpays for it:

The FY2025 EV/Sales ratio is ~21.61x, and it's ~3.4 times below the FY2024 ratio of 72.84x. In FY2026, assuming that the current consensus is right, NBIS should become an 11.5x EV/Sales stock, and I sincerely think that the current FY2026 sales consensus is too conservative. If it's adjusted by 10% to the upside, which is possible if the management's execution stays on the same level, NBIS will effectively start to trade at ~10.46x EV/Sales. Going beyond fiscal 2026, we should see another strong YoY performance as the planned capacity goes on line, and that should stop Nebius Group's valuation premium from falling by too much, in my view.

At 12x FY2026 sales and 10x FY2027 sales, NBIS can grow by 15% over the next 12 months and then add another 45-47% by the end of 2027, if my math turns out to be close to reality. That's why I see no other choice but to leave my bullish stance in place for now, especially after such a great Q2 print.

Risks And Your Takeaway

As I already noticed in my July article on NBIS, it's often impossible to value a fast-growing business, especially if it's just getting started to bring in meaningful revenue figures, with its profitability being on the horizon. I'm left with dealing with some basic stats to make a valuation analysis based on some forward-looking assumptions, and it's kind of risky because the analytical base, the toolkit, is limited. I admit that. In reality, over the next 12-24 months, anything can happen, and Nebius may eventually lose its competitiveness over time, or the overall AI infrastructure stops being such a hot topic for tech investors (hence, the valuation multiples can shrink again).

But despite these risks, I think that growth investors should primarily see the risk-reward story behind just valuation, as they typically do. Most growth investors know that Amazon (AMZN) used to run a deeply unprofitable business model until Bezos proved that it was a temporary thing, and the firm eventually reinvented the commerce market.

I'm not saying that Nebius is like Amazon in its '90s period - it would be too reckless to say and compare. But I think that Nebius Group, its management team, is doing everything right, and they're scaling the business well across different niches and industries, having a nice exposure to a multi-billion dollar TAM (on a consolidated basis). And they're already close to EBITDA profitability.

I think that NBIS should keep its valuation premium in place for as long as it shows superior growth rates (for the next few years), and that's why I remain bullish on the stock's prospects.

Good luck with your investments!

data.title: Regal Rexnord: Struggling Along
data.description:
In the summer of last year, I observed a leverage overhang in the case of shares of Regal Rexnord (NYSE:RRX) as organic growth slowed down. While the business has undergone a real transformation in recent years, it has come with debt taken on, which combined with a sluggish operating performance has created a debt overhang ever since.

Over the past year, Regal has made relatively little progress to reduce net debt, all while the stagnant operational performance has created somewhat of a lost year here. This makes me compelled to shares given a modest earnings multiple applied to the business, yet cautious amidst debt employed and softer organic performance displayed.

Power, Transmit & Control Motion Solutions

Founded in 1955 and headquartered in Milwaukee, Regal Rexnord is a near $6 billion industrial giant which employs 30,000 workers with activities reported across three segments.

The largest of these segments is a $2.6 billion transmission motion business, which is not just the largest with over 40% of sales, it is the most profitable as well with EBITDA margins seen in the mid-twenties. Products produced in this business include bearings, couplings, gearings, powertrains etc. applied in general industrial settings, metals & mining, energy, HVAC.

The remaining businesses are a bit smaller, each reporting $1.6 billion in sales. This includes a control motion business which posts margins in the low-twenties, including products like linear actuators, servo motors as well as drives & systems, served to automation markets, food & beverage, aerospace, industrial and medical applications.

Power motion is the third least most important business, generating a similar $1.6 billion in sales and EBITDA margins in the mid-teens. These include efficiency air moving systems and efficiency motors used in general commercial and HVAC applications.

The business has nearly doubled in terms of sales from pre-pandemic levels, from just over $3 billion at the time to about $6 billion today, driven by M&A efforts which however resulted in 50% shareholder dilution over time, while adding quite some net debt as well. That being said, the business has undergone a dramatic transformation, through which the company has grown substantially and has become more diversified as well.

Valuation Thoughts

The transformation of Regal Rexnord has been driven by a $4.95 billion deal to acquire Altra Industrial Motion, a deal which closed early in 2023, but at the same time pushed up leverage to 4 times EBITDA. This was in part offset by a $400 million divestment of Industrial Motors and Generator activities, of course a much smaller divestment.

Early in 2024, the company reported a 29% increase in full-year sales to $6.25 billion, with organic growth reported at 7%. GAAP operating profits of $377 million were entirely eaten (and some more) by a $431 million interest expense. Adjusted earnings were reported at $9.15 per share; that number is actually down from a $10.75 per share number in 2022.

Net debt was very substantial at $5.75 billion, for a 4.1 times leverage ratio, as the company guided for adjusted earnings in 2024 to come in at a midpoint of $10.15 per share, amidst organic growth, synergies from the Altra deal, offset by the impact of the smaller divestment as well. After a 7% fall in first quarter sales for 2024, the earnings guidance was trimmed to a midpoint of $10 per share, created some anxiety among investors as leverage ratios stills surpassed 4 times.

This made me upbeat onto the business in the long haul, but cautious in the near term uncertainty.

Trading Flat

Since last summer, shares of Regal have traded in a $90-$180 range, with the lows seen this spring amidst tariff anxiety and highs set amidst market optimism seen around the time of the US presidential election last fall.

Forwarding to early this year, Regal reported a near 4% fall in full-year sales to $6.03 billion, driven by the divestment of some activities and weak operating performance of the business at large, as adjusted earnings actually ended up falling $0.03 to $9.12 per share, with GAAP earnings meaningfully lower due to various restructuring costs, but moreover amortization charges. One of the few positives was that net debt came down to $5.06 billion, and with EBITDA pressured to $1.31 billion, leverage ratios improved minimally to 3.9 times.

However, modest improvements were seen heading into 2025, with earnings seen advancing to a midpoint of $10 per share, more or less in line with the original guidance for 2024.

In March, the company updated the market by indicating that the gross annual unmitigated cost impact from tariffs on place was seen around $60 million, equal to about 1% of sales, with plans being made in place to mitigate the impact of these on the business.

In May, the company reported an 8% fall in first quarter sales to $1.42 billion, this hardly being a reassuring announcement, if not that adjusted earnings ticked up $0.15 to $2.15 per share. Net debt ticked down further to $4.99 billion, with trailing EBITDA pressured to $1.30 billion, as the business reaffirmed the full-year guidance.

Early in August, second quarter sales were reported down 3% to $1.50 billion, with organic sales reported down a percent. Momentum was driven by strong organic growth at power efficiency solutions, offset by organic sales declines at the other segments. Adjusted earnings are reported up $0.19 to $2.48 per share.

Net debt was down to $4.53 billion, for a 3.5 times leverage ratio based on EBITDA down to $1.29 billion on a trailing basis. The dramatic progress in the reduction in leverage is a bit artificial, as the company obtained $368 million in proceeds from an accounts receivable securitization program.

The company maintained the midpoint of the full-year earnings guidance but narrowed the range, seen on sales of around $5.95 billion, despite the unmitigated tariff impact as of August having roughly doubled to about $125 million.

What Now?

The reality is that between last year and today, shares have been trading flattish here, as the business has seen a stagnant performance as well, with revenues and earnings being flat, to even down a bit.

Amidst all this, shares still trade at a non-demanding 14–15 times earnings multiple, but this comes amidst the lack of organic growth reported here, while leverage remains rather elevated as well, as the progress on deleveraging has been painfully slow, I must say. The progress in net debt reduction down to 3.5 times EBITDA is largely driven by the securitization program, as otherwise net debt has hardly come down at all in relative terms.

This makes me largely reiterate the conclusion from last year, although the situation is a bit more comforting here, with leverage down a bit here and the overall expectations in the market having risen considerably. That, however, means that while appeal is improving, I fail to pull the trigger here, as I am still erring cautious amidst debt employed and a mediocre operating performance.

data.title: Archer-Daniels-Midland: Biofuel And Commodity Cycles Will Pave The Way
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I rate Archer-Daniels-Midland (NYSE:ADM) with a Buy rating. I think there are several aspects that could have a positive impact on the company's stock price. Corn and soybean prices are the first aspect. The prices of these raw materials are approaching their lowest levels in ten years. Since commodity prices are cyclicals, corn and soybean prices could considerably increase in the upcoming years. Therefore, the revenues of ADM will benefit under this scenario.

The second aspect is that ADM has not reported more accounting issues in its intersegment operations. This problem affected the company's stock price in 2024. Without this issue, the market could positively reevaluate ADM's stock price. The other factor is that the U.S. administration has proposed higher biofuel quotas for oil refiners. If this proposal is approved, the corn price could increase in the upcoming months.

I forecasted ADM's EBITDA for 2025-2029 under two scenarios. In the first scenario, a positive one, ADM's EBITDA is expected to increase by 58.38% in 2029 compared to 2024. Meanwhile, the second scenario, which contemplates a bearish outlook, points out that the EBITDA will decrease by 21.35% in 2029 compared to 2024. As such, my estimations suggest that an increase in ADM's EBITDA is more likely than a decrease. However, agricultural commodity prices could decline even further, representing a risk factor. If this scenario occurs, the company's EBITDA will decline by a higher percentage than projected in the bearish outlook. A trade war between the U.S. and Brazil (or with China) could affect ADM's operations, another risk factor. When pondering all the positive and negative aspects of ADM, I still consider the company to be an interesting investment opportunity.

Overview

In my first ADM write-up, I rated the company with a hold rating. In that article, I implemented a discounted cash flow (DCF) model, which suggested that the company was undervalued by only 5.76%. Due to the small undervaluation, I thought it was prudent to assign a hold rating.

The other reason for my hold rating was the annual volume of commodities that ADM commercializes. As depicted in the following chart, since 2019, ADM has experienced a decline in the volume of commodities that it processes. In 2024, 77.77% of the company's revenues came from the Total Ag Services and Oilseeds Segment. This segment is engaged in commercializing commodities. Consequently, to obtain higher revenues —assuming that commodity prices are constant— the company has to commercialize a higher volume of commodities. If ADM cannot gain access to new markets, its revenues will fluctuate in the same proportion as the agricultural commodity prices.

From a global perspective, the production of corn and soybeans has grown. From the season 2019/2020 to 2025/2026, global corn production is expected to increase by 12.00%, while global soybean production is expected to gain 25.15%, as depicted in the following chart. As such, ADM is losing market share. In my view, ADM should commercialize more commodities to expand its operations.

Positive Aspect

Agricultural commodities will be part of society. Mankind will continue to use corn, soybeans, wheat, and other agricultural commodities. As such, this industry will continue to have a demand for the years to come. For instance, oil is threatened by the surge of electric cars and other renewable energy sources. The oil industry could not exist as we know it in the following 50 years. However, agricultural commodities do not have a similar threat.

According to the United Nations, the global population will continue to grow. In November 2022, it reached 8 billion people. It took 12 years to grow from 7 billion to 8 billion. The United Nations estimates that the population will reach 9 billion by 2037. By mid-2080, it projects 10.3 billion. As the population increases, the global demand for agricultural commodities should increase. Therefore, ADM will have more opportunities to expand its operations.

Commodity Prices

Agricultural commodities are cyclicals. They experience a period of high prices, followed by a period of low prices, and then lower prices again. Subsequently, this cycle continues over and over. The following chart shows the historical prices of corn and soybeans since 1995. It shows the aforementioned cycle. The current prices of these commodities are approaching those observed between 2014 and 2019. In other words, the prices of these commodities are reaching their lowest level in more than ten years.

Low corn and soybean prices are a disadvantage for ADM, since it indicates that the company will experience a decline in its revenues, reducing the firm's net income. However, it is an advantage at the same time. As commented on in this section, commodity prices are cyclical. Consequently, they may experience higher prices in the upcoming years. Therefore, ADM's revenues would benefit from this increase.

Projection

This section projects the EBITDA of ADM under two different scenarios. The goal is to obtain ADM's expected EBITDA for 2029. With this projection, it will help to shed light on whether the company represents an interesting investment opportunity or not.

I begin under the scenario of high commodity prices for the upcoming years. This year, the global soybean price has declined, on average, by 6.28% compared to 2024. Meanwhile, this year, the global corn price has increased, on average, by 10.21%. Due to these performances, I assume that ADM's AG Services and Oilseeds Segment will experience zero growth in 2025. Subsequently, I anticipate that the segment will experience a compound annual growth rate (OTC:CAGR) of 4% in its revenues from 2026 to 2029. At the end of the forecasted period, the revenues would reach $77,814 million, as depicted in the following table. On the other hand, I also stipulate that the Carbohydrate Solution Segment will have zero growth this year. In the Q4 2024 Earnings Transcripts, the company said that it expects a decline in the segment's revenues for 2025. As such, I think that zero growth is a reasonable assumption. For 2026-2029, I use a 5.2% CAGR. I took this rate from the Precedence Research firm. This firm projects that the global carbohydrate market will grow at a 5.2% CAGR from 2024 to 2034. Finally, I project the revenues of the Nutrition Segment implementing a 6.50% CAGR. I also took this growth rate from the Precedence Research firm. With these assumptions in place, the projection suggests that ADM is expected to reach $102,042 million in revenues by 2029. This figure is similar to the revenues reported by ADM in 2022.

For projecting the EBITDA, I assume that the company will report a 4.5% EBITDA margin in 2024, which is lower than the EBITDA margins reported between 2019 and 2024, as illustrated in the table below. Due to the fact that I hypothesize higher commodity prices for the upcoming years, I anticipate a 0.45% improvement in the EBITDA margin each year. For instance, in 2026, the EBITDA margin will be 4.50% + 0.45% = 4.95%. Consequently, in 2029, the company will reach an EBITDA margin of 6.30%, corresponding to an EBITDA of $6,429 million. These values are similar to those reported by the company in 2022, a year with high commodity prices. My estimation suggests that ADM's EBITDA is expected to increase by 58.38% compared to 2024.

Second Estimation

I move to the second scenario, in which my premise is lower agricultural commodity prices, lower revenues, and lower EBITDA margins. As depicted in the first chart below, global soybean production has been higher than global domestic consumption since the 2022/2023 season. In addition, the chart also reveals that there are no clear signs of a global corn shortage. Consequently, corn and soybean prices could experience low levels in the upcoming years.

The second chart above indicates that ADM has never reached an EBITDA margin below 4.5% since 2016. Therefore, I think that an EBITDA margin of 4.5% is a realistic assumption for a bearish outlook.

Moving to the revenues, I assume that ADM's AG Services and Oilseeds Segment will experience a decline of 10% and 15% in its revenues in 2025 and 2026, respectively. Meanwhile, from 2027 to 2029, I anticipate that the revenues will decline by 2% annually, as depicted in the following table. On the other hand, I employ the same assumptions commented on in the previous section to project the Carbohydrate Solutions Segment and Nutrition Segment revenues.

With the previous postulates, my estimations suggest that ADM's revenues will decline to $70,946 million. To provide context, in 2020, ADM's revenues were $64,355 million. Consequently, the projection for 2029 situates the revenues slightly higher than those of 2020. When using a 4.5% EBITDA margin ((discussed before)), ADM's EBITDA is expected to decline to $3,193 million in 2029, as depicted in the following table. Comparing the previous result to the 2024 EBITDA, the projection for 2029 is 21.35% lower.

To conclude, in the previous section, my estimation suggests that the EBITDA could increase by 58.38% in 2029 compared to 2024. This estimation assumes higher agricultural commodity prices. Meanwhile, in this section, my projection suggests that ADM's EBITDA is expected to decline by 21.35%. The previous figure assumes lower agricultural commodity prices. As such, the two forecasts suggest that it is more likely an increase in ADM's EBITDA than a decline.

Peer Analysis

This section implements a peer analysis between ADM and five companies. Bunge (BG) and Wilmar (OTCPK:WLMIF) are ADM's main competitors since they trade and process agricultural commodities. Meanwhile, Ingredion (INGR), Darling Ingredients (DAR), Conagra Brands (CAG), and General Mills (GIS) use agricultural commodities as input to make their final products. The following chart shows the financial ratios to analyze.

In the last year, ADM's stock price declined by 10.99%, slightly lower than the median ratio. Meanwhile, over a five-year period, its stock price increased by 47.56%, considerably higher than the median in the peer group. For this year, the analyst consensus expects that the company will pay a dividend yield of 3.73%, lower than the median expectation. However, this value is higher than the one reported by BG, its closest competitor.

The P/E, EV/EBITDA, and price-to-book ratios of ADM are similar to the median ratios in the peer group. As such, they indicate that there is no sign of undervaluation or overvaluation. Only the price-to-cash flow ratio suggests that ADM is undervalued, since the company has a 4.89 ratio ((the lowest value)), while the median ratio is 6.84.

Moving to the growth ratios, for this year, the analyst consensus expects a 2.26% decline in ADM's revenues, slightly higher than the median ratio. Meanwhile, over a five-year period, the company achieved a 5.45% CAGR in its revenues, which is higher than the median ratio. ADM has better revenue ratios than BG. For this year, the EBITDA of ADM is expected to decline by 11.67%, an aspect of concern.

On the other hand, the profitability metrics indicate that the company has the lowest EBITDA and net income margins among the peer group. In addition, its return on equity is 5.89%, the second-lowest value. When comparing ADM with BG, ADM has lower profit margins than BG. Finally, ADM has a 40.68% debt-to-equity ratio, the lowest value in the peer group. This value indicates that the company has lower debt levels than its peers.

To conclude, ADM's future performance depends on commodity prices. This year, the analyst consensus expects lower revenues and EBITDA for ADM. Implicitly, the previous forecasts suggest lower agricultural commodity prices for the upcoming years. However, if agricultural commodity prices increase, ADM will experience higher revenues and EBITDA. In addition, the company will experience an improvement in its profitability ratios.

Biofuel

In June, the U.S. administration proposed higher quotas for the amount of biofuel that oil refiners are required to blend over the following two years. The proposal anticipates 24.02 billion gallons in 2026, for a 7.57% annual increase. In addition, it contemplates 24.46 billion gallons in 2027, representing a 9.54% increase compared to 2024. To provide context, on June 12, 2025, the WASDE report projected 5,500 billion bushels of ethanol & by-products for the 2025/2026 season. This figure represents 43.02% of the expected total use in the U.S. If this proposal is approved, the U.S. demand for corn will increase. Ultimately, it could push corn prices up, which will have a positive effect on ADM's revenues.

Accounting Issue

ADM's price has been affected due to some accounting practices with respect to certain intersegment transactions. These transactions altered the intersegment sales and operating profits. For instance, the Nutrition Segment reported $458 million in operating profits in the first nine months of 2023, before the adjustments. With the accounting adjustments, the real operating profit was $427 million. Consequently, the Nutrition Segment's operating profit was $31 million lower than that reported in the 2023 Annual Report, as depicted in the following table. The point is that ADM overestimated Nutrition's operating profits.

However, when adding the adjustments made to the operating profit of the three segments, they total zero. The company said that the adjustments do not have an impact on its balance sheet, income statement, and cash flow statements. Due to the inconsistency in the intersegment operations, the company placed its Chief Financial Officer and Senior Vice President, Vikram Luthar, on administrative leave in January 2024. ADM still reported misclassified intersegment transactions in the third quarter of 2024. All these events had an adverse effect on the company's stock price. Nonetheless, it seems that the company has left behind these headwinds. As such, its market capitalization could improve in the upcoming months.

Risk

Agricultural commodity prices could decline even further than the assumptions implemented in this article. If this scenario occurs, ADM's revenues and earnings could experience a significant decline. The second factor of risk is that the accounting issues that the company experienced in 2024 could continue to occur. At the beginning of 2024, the company reported miscalculations in its intersegment transactions. In the third quarter of 2024, it again presented problems with its intersegment operations. This problem may appear again this year, or in 2026.

The U.S. has conducted a policy of higher tariffs on other countries. For instance, it has implemented higher levies on Brazil and China. Brazil is the largest soybean producer in the world, and ADM has a significant presence in that country. As such, ADM could be in the middle of the dispute. For instance, the Brazilian government could retaliate against ADM.

Conclusion

In light of the analysis presented in this article, I consider ADM to be an excellent investment opportunity. Corn and soybean prices have declined and are approaching their lowest value in ten years. Since the prices of these commodities are cyclical, they could increase in the upcoming years. Consequently, the revenues of ADM will improve if the prices of these raw materials grow.

The company is no longer affected by its accounting issues experienced in 2024. That year, it misclassified its intersegment transactions. ADM's stock price was negatively affected by this problem in 2024. Consequently, if this issue does not occur again, the market capitalization of ADM could increase in the upcoming months.

I projected the EBITDA under a scenario of favorable agricultural commodity prices. My estimation suggests that ADM's EBITDA could reach $6,429 million in 2029. This value is 58.38% higher than its realization in 2024. In addition, I projected the EBITDA of ADM under a scenario of low commodity prices. My projection points out that the EBITDA could decline to $3,193 million in 2029. This value is 21.35% lower than the 2024 EBITDA. Nonetheless, a risk factor is that agricultural commodity prices could decline even further. If this occurs, the company could report financial losses. Finally, ADM is an American company. With an eventual trade war between the U.S. and foreign countries, the international operations of ADM may be negatively affected. When taking all of these aspects into consideration, I still consider ADM to be an excellent investment opportunity
